mapping_name: v4 to v6 Terminology & Feature Mapping
description: Complete mapping of checkout.js v4 terminology, button labels, funding sources, and features to v6 JS SDK equivalents

button_labels.description: v4 used style.label to configure button appearance, v6 uses session types and components

label_paypal.migration_notes: Standard PayPal button. Most common v4 button type.

label_checkout.migration_notes: Button text was different in v4, but functionality is the same as PayPal button in v6.

label_buynow.v4_description: Displays 'Buy Now' button with PayPal logo
label_buynow.migration_notes: Button text was different in v4, but functionality is the same as PayPal button in v6.

label_pay.migration_notes: Generic payment button. Maps to standard PayPal session in v6.

label_credit.migration_notes: V4 'Credit' is now 'Pay Later' in v6. Must check eligibility first.

breaking_changes: v6 does not support style.label configuration
v6 uses session types instead of button labels
Button appearance is controlled by eligibility and merchant configuration
Text customization is not available in v6 (follows PayPal branding guidelines)

funding_sources.description: v4 used funding.allowed/disallowed to enable payment methods, v6 uses components and session types

FUNDING_PAYPAL.v4_description: PayPal account payments (default, cannot be disabled)
FUNDING_PAYPAL.migration_notes: PayPal is always available. No need to explicitly enable.

FUNDING_CREDIT.v4_usage: Must be explicitly enabled in funding.allowed
FUNDING_CREDIT.migration_notes: V4 'Credit' terminology changed to 'Pay Later' in v6. Requires eligibility check.

FUNDING_VENMO.v4_usage: Constant existed but was NOT functional
FUNDING_VENMO.v4_support: NOT SUPPORTED in v4 (per official feature matrix)
FUNDING_VENMO.v4_reality: FUNDING.VENMO constant existed in code but Venmo payments did not work
FUNDING_VENMO.migration_notes: Venmo was NOT supported in v4 despite constant existing. V6 has full native support. This is a NEW feature, not a migration.

FUNDING_CARD.v4_usage: Must be explicitly enabled in funding.allowed
FUNDING_CARD.migration_notes: Card payments require separate component in v6.

FUNDING_ELV.migration_notes: APMs handled through standard PayPal session in v6.

breaking_changes: v6 does not have funding.allowed or funding.disallowed configuration
v6 requires separate components for each payment method
v6 requires eligibility checks before rendering buttons
v6 components must be specified during SDK initialization

api_endpoints.description: v4 primarily used v1 Payments API, v6 uses v2 Orders API

alternative_v4_pattern.description: v4 also supported v2 Orders API (less common)

client_side.onApprove: Sends order ID to server for capture
client_side.note: No client-side API calls in v6

migration_notes: If using v4 actions.payment.create() → Must migrate to v2 Orders API
If using v4 actions.order.create() → Server endpoints may need minimal changes
v6 eliminates client-side API calls for better security
All order creation and capture happens server-side in v6

feature_terminology.description: Product and feature name changes from v4 to v6

pay_later_credit.notes: Terminology changed from 'Credit' to 'Pay Later' to reflect expanded financing options

venmo.v4_reality: FUNDING.VENMO constant existed but feature did not work
venmo.notes: Venmo was NOT supported in checkout.js v4. This is a NEW feature in v6, not a migration.

vault.v6_feature: Full vault support with setup tokens and payment tokens
vault.notes: Vault functionality was not available in checkout.js v4

marks.description: Logo placements for alternative payment display

v6_options.note: v6 has limited customization - buttons follow PayPal branding guidelines
v6_options.available: Layout and presentation mode configuration only
v6_options.customization: Minimal - PayPal controls button appearance based on context

breaking_changes: v6 does not support label customization
v6 does not support extensive color/shape customization
v6 buttons auto-adapt based on eligibility and merchant configuration
v6 prioritizes consistent branding over customization

v4_pattern.name: Client-side rendering and execution
steps: Include checkout.js script with client ID
Configure paypal.Button.render() with style and funding
Implement payment() callback to create payment (client or server)
Implement onAuthorize() callback to execute payment
characteristics: Can create payments client-side using actions.payment.create()
Can create payments server-side and return payment ID
Execution can happen client-side or server-side
Minimal server requirements for basic integration

v6_pattern.name: Server-mediated integration
steps: Server generates client token with credentials
Client loads v6 SDK and initializes with client token
Client creates session (PayPal, Venmo, Pay Later, etc.)
Server creates order and captures payment
characteristics: All API calls happen server-side only
Client never handles credentials or payment creation
Enhanced security with client token authentication

migration_impact: Server-side endpoints are required for v6 (no client-side API calls)
Client token generation must be implemented
Eligibility checks must be added for conditional rendering
Payment flow changes from client-executed to server-mediated

components_and_products.description: Component/product naming in v4 vs v6
components_and_products.v4_note: Venmo was NOT supported in v4 despite FUNDING.VENMO constant existing

quick_reference.description: Quick lookup for common v4 → v6 migrations

venmo_button.v4: NOT SUPPORTED (FUNDING.VENMO constant existed but didn't work)
venmo_button.note: This is a NEW feature in v6, not a migration from v4

key_terminology_changes: Button labels (checkout, buynow, pay) → removed in v6
summary.architectural_shift: V4 allowed client-side payment operations, v6 requires server-mediated flow for enhanced security
summary.feature_expansion: V6 adds Vault, Subscriptions, Hosted Fields, and full APM support not available in v4
summary.customization_reduction: V6 reduces button customization in favor of consistent PayPal branding and auto-adaptation
